If this gets merged, one can add support to windows with the extra modifiers by updating `ConsoleInputReader._handle_mouse` in `win32.py` (partial implementation as an example):
```py
    def _handle_mouse(self, ev: MOUSE_EVENT_RECORD) -> List[KeyPress]:
        FROM_LEFT_1ST_BUTTON_PRESSED = 0x0001
        RIGHTMOST_BUTTON_PRESSED =  0x0002
        FROM_LEFT_2ND_BUTTON_PRESSED = 0x0004

        RIGHT_ALT_PRESSED = 0x0001
        LEFT_ALT_PRESSED = 0x0002
        RIGHT_CTRL_PRESSED = 0x0004
        LEFT_CTRL_PRESSED = 0x0008
        SHIFT_PRESSED = 0x0010

        MOUSE_MOVED = 0x0001
        MOUSE_WHEELED = 0x0004

        # Event type
        if ev.EventFlags & MOUSE_MOVED:
            event_type = MouseEventType.MOUSE_MOVE
        elif ev.EventFlags & MOUSE_WHEELED:
            if ev.ButtonState > 0:
                event_type = MouseEventType.SCROLL_UP
            else:
                event_type = MouseEventType.SCROLL_DOWN
        elif not ev.ButtonState:
            event_type = MouseEventType.MOUSE_UP
        else:
            event_type = MouseEventType.MOUSE_DOWN

        # Buttons
        if not ev.ButtonState:
            button = MouseButton.NO_BUTTON
        elif ev.ButtonState & FROM_LEFT_1ST_BUTTON_PRESSED:
            button = MouseButton.LEFT
        elif ev.ButtonState & RIGHTMOST_BUTTON_PRESSED:
            button = MouseButton.RIGHT
        # More buttons here:
        #     https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/console/mouse-event-record-str?redirectedfrom=MSDN
        # For now, just assume middle-mouse button.
        else:
            button = MouseButton.MIDDLE

        # Modifiers (If using IntFlag Enum for modifier keys)
        mods = 0
        if ev.ControlKeyState & LEFT_ALT_PRESSED or ev.ControlKeyState & RIGHT_ALT_PRESSED:
            mods |= MouseModifierKey.ALT
        if ev.ControlKeyState & LEFT_CTRL_PRESSED or ev.ControlKeyState & RIGHT_CTRL_PRESSED:
            mods |= MouseModifierKey.CONTROL
        if ev.ControlKeyState & SHIFT_PRESSED:
            mods |= MouseModifierKey.SHIFT

        modifier = MouseModifier(mods)
        ...
        # Package data somehow
        
 ```

Something I just came across while working on the metrics parser library and that we talked about as part of https://github.com/mozilla/metric-config-parser/pull/42

The [`client_id_column` and `submission_date` fields](https://github.com/mozilla/metric-config-parser/blob/91ee9f57e795719536f99b7f12add340a942d159/metric_config_parser/data_source.py#L122-L123) are defined as optional so they can (and will be null) for some of the data source definitions. So it is probably good to check for this null condition here and cast to a default value. 

I'll need to remove the default values from the metric parser library since they are causing some issues when overriding a few fields of an existing data source definition. In those cases the `client_id_column` and `submission_date` fields will _always_ get overridden, which is not always desired when the original definition should be kept.

Thanks for opening this RFC!

There are a few high-level things that I'm concerned about:
1. The `with_column()` column adding/mutating API isn't vectorized and is a pretty thin layer over `map_batches()`, so it's sure to be much slower than the `map_batches()` equivalent without a large improvement in UX.
2. The fact that each of these add/mutate/select/drop operations results in a separate `map_batches()` execution stage will make it much less efficient than the single `map_batches()` alternative.

For a concrete before/after comparison, here's the preprocessing stages from the big data training CUJ, along with an added bit to drop columns.

## Before RFC

```python
        def batch_transformer(df: pd.DataFrame):
            # Drop columns.
            df = df.drop(columns["bad_col_1", "bad_col_2"])

            # Drop nulls.
            df = df.dropna(subset=["nullable_feature"])

            # Add new column.
            df["new_col"] = (
                df["feature_1"] - 2 * df["feature_2"] + df["feature_3"]
            ) / 3.0

            # Transform column.
            df["feature_1"] = 2.0 * df["feature_1"] + 0.1

            return df

        ds = ds.map_batches(batch_transformer, batch_format="pandas")
```

## After RFC

```python
        ds = ds.drop_columns("bad_col_1", "bad_col_2") \
            .with_column(
                "new_col", lambda r: (r["feature_1"] - 2 * r["feature_2"] + r["feature_3"]) / 3.0) \
            .with_column("feature_1", lambda r: 2.0 * r["feature_1"] + 1) \
            .map_batches(lambda df: df.dropna(subset=["nullable_feature"]), batch_format="pandas")
```

I don't think that the latter is a big improvement in UX, and it will probably end up being a good bit slower. 🤔

Let's discuss this at our next meeting. The problem currently is that there are still a lot of necessary Python calls in the loop (e.g., `Stimulus`, `BiphasicPulse`), so you can't release the gil. This would explain why the speedup is limited... 

Making this work is tricky. You'd have to strip all the Python overhead and e.g. purely operate on arrays. However, currently the array changes shape on each iteration, which is also slow.

One way might be to:

1.  Do all the Python/error checks outside the Cython function
2.  Use p2p to create `BiphasicPulse` outside Cython. Then only pass the time & data arrays of the `BiphasicPulse` object
3.  Preallocate the time & data arrays of the final `Stimulus` that will be created
4.  Fill the values of the arrays in the loop without calling out to Python objects

thanks for doing this daniel. this looks like a pretty tricky piece of code, and it looks very clean! i'm OK with merging as is, even given the blocking request limitation to get a fix out, but this shouldn't stay this way.

fundamentally i think we need to solve the streaming proxy issue (i.e., proxy streamed requests without consuming the request, but allowing us to seek to 0 if needed) for this to work properly. then we could theoretically solve the body re-writing by wrapping the stream iterator that underlies the request object. this is pretty involved though and should maybe be shepherded by @alexrashed or me. happy to do this in a follow up PR.

an alternative for now would be what i suggest in the comment, to basically skip body re-writing for streamed bodies. but not sure that will work well :shrug:

1. Copy&Paste an existing method
2. Change the operation and fill in some random data
3. replace `doit` with `doit(True)` which dumps the generated hex
4. Take the hex and put it into `self.cm`
5. Run the test.

As you can see in `doit()` it compares 

* the generated hex against the hex in the unittest
* the unittest hex against the RPC hex
* the generated hex against the RPC hex

OK, it took me a few hours to dig through the whole caster construction (together with @bstaletic), but here I am with another review.

I think one of the things that was confusing to me is that these are 3 independent changes in one PR:

1. Changing the subcaster's `cast_op_type<type>` to `cast_op_type<type&>`

  OK, so you're asking the subcaster how to cast to a `type&` instead of to a `type`. The reason this is the case is that in almost all cases in pybind11, these two types would get the same conversion operator.
  Do you have a good example of when these would be different? This might be good to add in a test (see later remark about tests).
  I think it's OK to change though, since indeed, you actually want a `type&` from the subcaster.

2. Loosening the `static_cast` that does currently not allow for `subcaster::operator const type&()`.

   Fair enough, yes. The repetition of explicitly listing `const` seems a bit ugly, but the `std::remove_const` was already there, so ...
   An alternative would be something like `std::is_convertible<subcaster_cast_op_type, std::reference_wrapper<type>>::value`? But fine to keep as it is now, I think.

3. The `&` in `subcaster.operator subcaster_cast_op_type&()` was completely superfluous already before this PR, I believe (remember we just asserted that it was already an lvalue reference!). So it's a correct change, but independent from this PR.


Now, as I said before: what bothers me a bit is that tests are added, but these tests already didn't fail before the actual changes of this PR. Normally, tests added in a PR would fail before the function changes of the PR?
So should we still add a minimal (!) test with a  `type_caster` that has an `operator const Foo &()`  that's different from `operator Foo &()`? And a `type_caster` that differentiates its `cast_op_type<Bar&>` from `cast_op_type<Bar>`? (do you have a good example of such a case, perhaps; in a smaller version than the full `FreezableInt` file?)

I'd also like to hear from @bstaletic if this summary and assessment makes sense, before officially approving.

vCD will not complain if the access level is reduced; however, if a user has full control access to a defined entity, a request to lower the access to read-write will receive a successful response code, but the access will remain at full control. Oh, I'd rather these options not be a part of ArrayBuilderOptions, since they only apply to JSON (as they work around a JSON limitation). ArrayBuilder is used in a lot of contexts other than JSON. If there's some reason why it would be very difficult to do this, then we can consider this an internal detail, since it's not exposed to the Python interface to ArrayBuilder, but it's looking to me like a bad direction to grow.

The reason we have ArrayBuilderOptions is because ArrayBuilder makes a tree of indeterminate depth. At any moment, it could encounter new data that broadens the type of its array, so each node needs to have access to the configuration options, which includes such things as "how big should the initial allocation be?"

These JSON options don't need to be pushed down this way. The JSON-handling code is all in a SAX parser class that doesn't create nodes of a tree (it calls methods on ArrayBuilder, which internally builds those nodes). So the JSON options don't have to be "pushed down" in the same way. That's why they don't really belong in ArrayBuilderOptions.

Also, about the options themselves: users need to be able to specify the spelling of `"nan"`, `"inf"`, and `"-inf"`, since there's no standard—different JSON writers generate different strings: `"NaN"`, `"Inf"`, `"-Inf"`, `"Infinity"`, `"-Infinity"`, and `"minusInfinity"` are all things I've seen. A single dataset won't have multiple spellings, so these are three new options:

   * the string that gets mapped to `std::numeric_limits<double>::quiet_NaN()`
   * the string that gets mapped to `std::numeric_limits<double>::infinity()`
   * the string that gets mapped to `-std::numeric_limits<double>::infinity()`

any of which can be None (i.e. _no_ string that gets mapped to a numeric limit).

If I grok this correctly, the problem is that `make_file_id` is not aware of the `overwrite_mode` of the workspace and should behave differently. It should not try to avoid clashes because that would lead to inconsistent state, i.e. more files with different IDs instead of overwriting existing files.

One solution could be to add a new kwarg `overwrite` to the `make_file_id` method. Not ideal because that requires all the procesors to be adapted and can lead to easy-to-miss inconsistencies across processors.

Since `overwrite` is a state of the `OcrdWorkspace` class and there is no link back from `OcrdMets` to the workspace it represents, we cannot go `OcrdFile` -> `OcrdMets` -> `OcrdWorkspace` - but maybe this should be possible. We could add a reference from OcrdMets to OcrdWorkspace in the latter's `__init__`.

Then we could check `OcrdFile.mets.workspace.overwrite_mode` and skip the loop to create unique IDs.

Hey thanks for writing up a draft real quick! I got quite a bit of feedback so let's get started:

> RE: Folder vs single document

I agree a single page FAQ would be better. The good news is that I found a RST directive that almost does what we want. It produces a nice TOC in the style of the TOCTREE but for the current document. Unfortunately the default styling with the current theme is IMO ugly:

![Screenshot from 2021-05-22 17-36-06](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/63936253/119241430-3e196300-bb24-11eb-858c-9e9c27bc2859.png)

Thankfully it's quite easy to fix this with some custom CSS:

```css
.contents.topic {
  background: none;
  border: none;
}
```

![Screenshot from 2021-05-22 17-40-37](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/63936253/119241512-d7e11000-bb24-11eb-91c7-4d0df8ab8833.png)

We can add this CSS to `docs/_static/custom.css` which gets picked up by Sphinx.

Here's the markup you'll need to use this directive (yes, I still prefer MyST over RST):

````md
```{contents}
:local:
:backlinks: none
```
````

OR in reStructuredText:

```rst
.. contents::
  :local:
  :backlinks: none
```

> RE: Top level placement

I was more thinking putting it at the end of the "user guide" section, so after the "Guides" section. It's IMO a bit jarring to have a "Frequently Asked Questions" right after "Usage and Configuration".

Nice to see some progress on this! A few suggestions:

* It's likely worth allowing chunked upload for all files, with a `--multi-part` switch (or similar). We've many users in slow networking environments, and it'd benefit them in the event of a connection disconnect. Exposing this, and the chunk size (but falling back to a size check and defined chunk size if arguments aren't provided) feels like it would help!
* Could we perform the chunked upload within `files.py`? It might only need small changes to the headers provided in `request_file_upload`. Additionally, the chunking logic could loop there too. Generally favor keeping the logic for the interactions with the service decoupled from the commands modules.
* On the back of that, would really favor leveraging the cloudsmith API bindings to perform interactions with the service. Multi-part _should_ be available in the current version of the bindings, but if not, raise the bat signal to one of us and we can look too 😄 
* This one is NAB (Not a blocker) - but it might be possible to pass the file itself into a memory stream, and create memory views for each chunk. It'd be possible to potentially parallelize the chunk uploads then, using futures or equivalent. This is just an idea, but I don't think it's 100% required - might not even be possible as we also support Python 2.x to this day in the CLI.

Before we can accept it, there are some things that need fixing:

* All the snapshots (files ending with .snapshot.json) are automatically created by executing the tests against AWS and automatically recorded. Please follow the guide here: https://docs.localstack.cloud/contributing/parity-testing/ for more information, and please do not hesitate to ask here or in the #contributing community slack channel if you have any questions.
* All files in `localstack/aws/api` are automatically created from the botocore definitions. Therefore, manual additions are not allowed. Once the botocore update PR is merged, we can regenerate the lambda API, which will fix this.
* Please add the python 3.10 runtime to the python runtimes in `tests/integration/awslambda/conftest.py`, in the `RUNTIMES_AGGREGATED` dict. This will allow the tests in `test_lambda_common.py` to be executed with the new runtime, which provides several testing scenarios across all supported runtimes. Please also run those tests with a snapshot update, as we need the python3.10 snapshots as well, then.

Sorry for being so nitpicky, but we make a lot of effort to maintain AWS parity, and the automatically generated snapshots are a major tool for this, so we need to rely on those.

You can read more here: https://docs.github.com/en/github/managing-your-work-on-github/linking-a-pull-request-to-an-issue#linking-a-pull-request-to-an-issue-using-a-keyword. This makes sure any issue that should be closed are in fact closed (and I won't have to go proactively hunt for such issues!), keeping our very chaotic issue tracker ever so slightly less chaotic and scary :) **Although, if this PR doesn't end up covering IndentationError, closing the issue might not be the right call.**

Thanks for the PR! It's great to see this getting closer to being fixed. I looked over your work and I have a few suggestions and requests to make, so let's get started:

It would be extra fantastic to catch and print out a prettier error message for IndentationError as well. The main difficulty would probably be getting the line no. and col. information out of the exception object since this exception is a built-in exception and has a different argument setup than TokenError (or ParseError) which are custom. Although I wouldn't mind ignoring IndentationError because the current error message isn't that bad actually:

```console
~ via Python v3.8.5 took 1m10s232ms 
123❯ cat test.py
def t():
    hello = "world"

  a = 123

~ via Python v3.8.5 
1❯ black test.py --check
error: cannot format test.py: unindent does not match any outer indentation level (<tokenize>, line 4)
Oh no! 💥 💔 💥
1 file would fail to reformat.
```

Here's the only place where IndentationError is raised in blib2to3: https://github.com/psf/black/blob/1bedc176d19f0e71c24149c696c5a2a71f21e941/src/blib2to3/pgen2/tokenize.py#L522

Finally, you'll need a changelog entry in `CHANGES.md` that describes what your patch does. Don't forget to add your PR number to the end. While you're doing that, you can also add yourself to the `AUTHORS.md` file!

Oh, by the way, you can use special keywords in the PR description so GitHub will automatically close an issue upon merge. You can read more here: https://docs.github.com/en/github/managing-your-work-on-github/linking-a-pull-request-to-an-issue#linking-a-pull-request-to-an-issue-using-a-keyword. This makes sure any issue that should be closed are in fact closed (and I won't have to go proactively hunt for such issues!), keeping our very chaotic issue tracker ever so slightly less chaotic and scary :) **Although, if this PR doesn't end up covering IndentationError, closing the issue might not be the right call.**

Thanks @bukepo for this. At first I was supportive of adding this, but the more I think on this, the more I feel the use of `&&` may not be well-suited for indicating ownership transfer:
- Passing a `&&` to a function/method, indicates to it that the object is an R-value
- An R-value typically indicates that the reference is to a temporary object (which will be gone).
- It hints to the method/function that if it wants to copy the object, it can reuse and take over the resources/content of the passed object reference (it does not need to copy its content)
- So passing a `&&` to a function/method does not necessarily indicate that caller is passing the ownership.

Smart pointer and/or smart reference are better indicator of ownership. As I mentioned in our internal sync I have a branch to add the notion of `OwnerPtr` to use with `Message` ([branch](https://github.com/abtink/openthread/commit/2fa7c56c62e5c4758e6d2e929d11738ddca50c50)). It is on hold due to its code size overhead.

--------------

With regards to `Message` itself, one idea is to use `Message &` vs `const Message &` to indicate whether the message ownership is being passed to the method/function.
- I liked this model, but if I recall correctly, there was a challenge with it (requires some additional changes)
- I think in some functions/methods we pass a `Message &` even though the method only reads the content of the message (example of this is `Lowpan::Compress()` methods). 
- The reason for passing `Message &` seems to be that the method wants to `SetOffset()` on the message. 
- If we somehow decouple the changing of offset from the `Message` I think we may be able to use `Message &` and `const Message &` to indicate ownership transfer.

Let's do the following changes before we merge this:
Move `ray.serialization.register_serializer` to `ray.util.register_serializer` and a note into the docs that it is `Experimental` for now. Also make the API like this:
```
ray.util.register_serializer(A, serializer=custom_serializer, deserializer=custom_deserializer)
```

I've played with the example and looked through most of the code and I'm super impressed! Great job Lisa! I wouldn't change very much, just the default behaviour for old users and introduce a very conservative suggestion for the ``global_constraints``.

## Naming schemes

I agree with your naming: we need ``investment_periods`` to distinguish from ``snapshot_periods`` that run through the year. Old PyPSA "overnight" behaviour is often called "multi-period" optimisation, but we need to make clear that we also do multiple investment periods here.

## Default behaviour

The guiding principle is that old users shouldn't have to make any big changes, and that there should be no surprises.

I suggest that n.snapshots stays the same, and can be either MultiIndex or Index.

All PyPSA functions should by default treat MultiIndex snapshots just the same as Index snapshots. This is the case for old PyPSA ``n.lopf(pyomo=False)``, but ``n.lopf(pyomo=True)``, ``n.lpf()`` and ``n.pf()`` all break when using MultiIndex - we should fix this in future releases (but not now).

The multi-period optimisation is the only one to treat MultiIndex differently, and must be activated with:

``n.lopf(pyomo=False, multi_investment_periods=True)``

When this is activated, it checks that ``n.snapshots`` is MultiIndex, that level 0 is integer, and that the integers are increasing. It also checks that all investment periods appear in the index of ``n.investment_period_weightings`` and vice-versa, throwing an error if not.

If ``n.lopf(pyomo=True, multi_investment_periods=True)`` is called, an error is thrown. By default ``multi_investment_periods=False``.

### Snapshot weightings

This is a parallel change to the investment periods. In PyPSA Version 0.18.0 we will replace the pd.Series ``n.snapshot_weightings`` with a pd.DataFrame with three columns ``[objective_weightings, generator_weightings, store_weightings]``. This separates out the three current use cases of weightings in the LOPF:

- ``objective_weightings``: for operational costs in the objective function
- ``store_weightings``: for elapsed time in the filling of ``Store`` and ``StorageUnit`` components
- ``generator_weightings``: for the contribution of generators to ``GlobalConstraint`` components for e.g. CO2 budgets

Example use cases:

- 3-hourly sampling for full year: ``objective_weighting = generator_weighting = store_weighting = 3``.
- hourly sampling for 10 days to represent full year with cyclic storage: ``objective weighting = generator_weighting = 52. , 
store_weighting = 1.``

BUT this case fails if e.g. a ``Store`` represents non-cyclic depletion of e.g. biomass or some other finite resource, in which case you want ``store_weighting = 52``, like ``generator_weighting``. This interface needs all storage doing short-term cycling, or all storage non-cyclic (depleting a finite resource). We should fix this when we implement Kotzur's seasonal storage scheme.

### Investment period weightings

I think the way Lisa implemented this looks good, maybe change the names of weightings to be similar to the ``snapshot_weightings``?

I also suggest not to have ``n.investment_periods``, but track these only via level 0 of ``n.snapshots``.

### Behaviour when an empty network is generated

If you call ``n = pypsa.Network()`` by default will be created:

- ``n.snapshots = pd.Index(["now"])``
- ``n.snapshot_weightings = pd.DataFrame([[1.,1.,1.]],index=["now"],
                                     columns=["objective_weightings","store_weightings","generator_weightings"])``
- ``n.investment_period_weightings = pd.DataFrame(columns=["objective_weightings","store_weightings","generator_weightings"])``

### Behaviour when a network from PyPSA Version < 0.18.0 is imported

Create new empty ``n.investment_period_weightings`` like for empty network above.

The values of pd.Series ``n.snapshot_weightings`` are copied across all three values of the new pd.DataFrame ``n.snapshot_weightings``.

## Global constraints for ``p_nom_max`` at each bus, carrier and investment_period

I suggest to keep these all in ``n.global_constraints`` since they are global in the sense of affecting multiple components at once. We need to add to the ``GlobalConstraint`` table attributes ``bus``, ``carrier`` and ``investment_period`` and type ``multi_investment_period_p_nom_max`` or similar.

Yes, this will create lots of global constraints, but introducing e.g. ``n.global_constraints_p`` doesn't help since you need 3-d information.

This way is minimally invasive.

## Treatment of sub_networks

I think the current way is good. The sub_networks are generated as needed, and the last one to be generated is stored. There is no need to store them from all investment periods, i.e. no need for ``n.sub_networks_p``.

The sub_network is anyway more of a hidden variable.

## Storage behaviour between investment periods

Did I understand correctly that cyclic storage is cyclic within each investment period? This behaviour would be fine.

If the storage is not cyclic, is energy transfered between investment periods? Probably this is not such a good idea for e.g. depletable biomass. But maybe we should model depletable biomass as a generator, rather than as a store that starts full. Then the generator can fill the storage at the start of each year?

## Check objective function

Since the objective function is quite complicated now, please check for some non-trivial examples that the Gurobi objective function is also what you calculated from the results. This should help to find bugs.

## Coding style

Try to avoid abbreviations like ``inv_p`` and ``sns`` in API functions like ``network.determine_network_topology``. Here we should be explicit for understandability. You can abbreviate inside functions, that's fine.

Also try to avoid committing code very specific to your own setup, like paths to libraries.

And also, we need to decide whether we should execute Weka CLI on quota setting or not. Because setting a quota for the virtual folder via API if the virtual folder is not empty is not allowed, but is only available in CLI.

There are two approaches to solving this problem.
* **Approach 1. Set default quota when creating virtual folder.**
   In this case, the default quota would be the min value among two values described below:
   - Max size of vfolder in keypair resource policy that current user's crendential are belonged.
   - Overall size (allowed) of weka storage
   
   Pros: Easy to apply and implement.
   Cons: May ruin the "degree of freedom" of quota setting, since It always set quota.


* **Approach 2. Set folder empty checking condition, so when quota via API if vfolder is empty, and execute CLI if it's not empty.**
   This approach will require login (with session) to weka cluster when executing storage-proxy.
   
   Pros: Users (superadmin role) can set a quota whenever they want, they don't need to set quota every time.
   Cons: A bit trickier compare to Approach 1.

I don't consider this to be a strict breaking change, however, I don't think this is something I should do because the range of emoji tends to be volatile between unicode versions and that's a maintenance burden historically I didn't want to deal with. That aside, the range in this regex is incredibly wide to the point of being meaningless for its purpose.

Consider a sentence in Japanese:

```py
>>> import re
>>> regex = re.compile(r'^[\u0023-\u0039]?[\u00ae\u00a9\U00002000-\U0010ffff]+$')
>>> regex.match('お前は何してんだ？')
<re.Match object; span=(0, 9), match='お前は何してんだ？'>
```

The reason why this regex works with everything is because it casts such a wide net, basically a large amount of the BMP is being included here. A more proper solution would be to take into consideration the actual ranges that a unicode emoji falls under, and this is very much a best-guess basis. The known ranges, from looking around, are more or less:

- `U+1F1E6` to `U+1F1FF`: Flags
- `U+1F300` to `U+1F5FF`: Misc Symbols and Pictographs
- `U+1F600` to `U+1F64F`: Emoticons
- `U+1F680` to `U+1F6FF`: Transport and Map
- `U+1F900` to `U+1F9FF`: Supplemental Symbols and Pictographs
- `U+2600` to `U+26FF`: Misc Symbols
- `U+2700` to `U+27BF`: Dingbats
- `U+FE00` to `U+FE0F`: Variation Selectors

These ranges are inclusive.

```shell
cmake -DBUILD_CPP_TEST=ON -DUSE_OPENMP=OFF -DUSE_DEBUG=ON ..
make testlightgbm -j4
./../testlightgbm
```

That's consistent with https://cmake.org/cmake/help/latest/manual/cmake-language.7.html#command-invocations, which says

Thanks for taking the time to dig into this. I've been bashing my head against this issue for a while. 

Adding some cooldowns seems totally reasonable, but I am worried they may be masking the underlying problem. The cooldowns added in this PR are also extremely long and may make clusters feel very unresponsive.

In the classic adaptive functionality the interval was 5 seconds and the scale down cooldown was 15 seconds. I would prefer things to be similar here. If the bugs are still presenting themselves at that speed then there is something deeper to investigate.

It's a good refactoring.

Though, `get_conatiner_process_table()` could be reimplemented using `/proc/{pid}/status`'s `NSPid` field (ref: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man5/proc.5.html) following `get_host_process_table()`, as long as we're on the Linux kernel 4.1 or later.
It would be better to avoid spawning subprocesses for this kind of simple queries whenever possible.

I think we could rewrite `get_container_process_table()` as a follow-up PR.

As you note, we don't do much validation in the SDK, this is for a few reasons that I know of:

1. The SDK is designed to be a fairly thin wrapper of the API, validation requires additional complexity and more frequent updates to keep aligned with the API
2. If we miss an API change and don't update the SDK accordingly, or users need to be using an older version for compatibility reasons, then they are stuck with incorrect or inconsistent SDK side validation.
3. The SDK is used in release-testing-tools to test API validation of bad inputs

That said, the one place I could maybe get behind more aggressive SDK side validation would be in helper classes like `FlowCreateRequest` as it is possible to work around points 2 and 3 by passing a custom dict to the client method. I think I would prefer leaving the SDK a fairly thin wrapper even in these classes though.

Usually when we encounter weirdness, it is either because `mypy` is unnecessarily strict or we actually have spaghetti / unsafe code that should be refactored. Given that the latter is usually not obvious, we opted to simply silence `mypy` where it complains, since the code works as is.
